mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers ocean distribution (A.1), physical/chemical properties of seawater (B), ocean currents (H), and ocean-atmosphere interactions (E).
Explores the molecular structure, hydrogen bonding, and anomalous properties of water, directly relevant to both seawater (B) and freshwater (C) chemistry.

mappings.vsiAuthor: David George Bowers, Emyr Martyn Roberts
mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers tides (G.3) and tidal currents (H.4.b), including the gravitational forces that generate them and their effects on ocean circulation.
Directly covers freshwater lake systems (A.2.a), their physical and chemical properties (C), and salinity and water balance.
Directly covers river systems (A.2.a), runoff in the hydrologic cycle (D.4), and water resources (D.6).
Directly covers glaciers, ice sheets (A.3.a), sea-level effects, and the role of ice in the hydrologic cycle (D.5).
Examines Pleistocene ice sheets and ice cores (A.3.a-b), glacial effects on sea levels, and the role of ice masses in hydrologic processes (D.5).
Explores Arctic sea ice, pack ice dynamics (A.3.b), and the cryosphere's influence on ocean circulation and global water balance.
Focuses on the Antarctic ice sheet (A.3.a), iceberg calving (A.3.b), and the Southern Ocean's role in thermohaline deep-sea circulation (H.4.a).
Addresses ocean acidification and the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation, linking biological processes to seawater properties (B) and ocean currents (H).
Explains ocean-atmosphere interactions including El Nino-Southern Oscillation (E.3.b), the Gulf Stream's climate effects (E.3.c), and radiation/heat budgets (E.1).
Examines the ocean as a carbon dioxide sink (E.2), sea-level changes from polar ice melt, and human-induced disruption of the global water balance (F).
Situates the global hydrologic cycle (D) and ocean-atmosphere CO2 exchange (E.2) within the broader framework of interconnected Earth system processes.
Applies wave physics to surface waves, ocean swells, and tsunamis (G.1) as well as internal waves (G.2).
Highlights how ocean water chemistry, warming, and acidification affect reef ecosystems, connecting seawater properties (B) to human impacts on the hydrosphere (F).
Describes the Gulf Stream conveyor belt system (H.3-H.4) and the ocean's role in redistributing heat globally through deep and surface currents.
Addresses tsunamis (G.1), coastal storm-surge flooding, and extreme hydrological events relevant to waves and human impacts (F-G).
